title: Singular Value Decomposition
description: Introduction Eigendecomposition is powerful, but it has a fatal flaw: it only works on square matrices. Real-world data matrices are rarely square. We might have m users and n movies, or m documents and n words. Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is the generalization that works on any matrix, regardless of shape. It is arguably the most important matrix factorization in applied linear algebra and machine learning. What SVD Reveals Rank: Number of non-zero singular values = rank of matrix. Range: Column space is spanned by left singular vectors (U). Null Space: Right singular vectors (V) with zero singular values span null space. Condition Number: Ratio of largest to smallest singular value measures numerical stability. Prerequisites SVD builds on eigendecomposition and orthogonality. Understanding PCA also helps, as PCA is often computed using SVD. The Movie Rating Intuition Consider a matrix A where rows are Users and columns are Movies. Entry A[i,j] is user i's rating of movie j. SVD factorizes this into three parts that reveal hidden structure: U (User-Concept) Each row describes a user in terms of hidden "concepts" (latent factors). "Alice: 0.9 Sci-Fi lover, 0.1 Romance fan, 0.3 Action enthusiast" ОЈ (Strength) Diagonal values show how "strong" each concept is in the dataset. "Sci-Fi concept explains 40% of variance, Romance 25%, Action 15%..." рқ‘ү рқ‘Ү V T (Concept-Movie) Each column describes a movie in terms of the same concepts. "The Matrix: 0.95 Sci-Fi, 0.05 Romance, 0.8 Action" To predict Alice's rating for The Matrix: multiply Alice's concept profile by concept strengths by Matrix's concept profile. The "concepts" are learned automatically from the data, not pre-defined! Mathematical Formulation SVD Theorem: For any real matrix A of size m Г— n, there exist orthogonal matrices U and V and a diagonal matrix ОЈ such that: рқҗҙ = рқ‘Ҳ ОЈ рқ‘ү рқ‘Ү A=UОЈV T рқ‘Ҳ U ( рқ‘ҡ Г— рқ‘ҡ mГ—m) Left Singular Vectors Orthogonal: рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘Ү рқ‘Ҳ = рқҗј U T U=I Columns are eigenvectors of рқҗҙ рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү AA T ОЈ ОЈ ( рқ‘ҡ Г— рқ‘ӣ mГ—n) Singular Values Diagonal with entries рқңҺ рқ‘– вүҘ 0 Пғ i вҖӢ вүҘ0 Sorted: рқңҺ 1 вүҘ рқңҺ 2 вүҘ вҖҰ вүҘ 0 Пғ 1 вҖӢ вүҘПғ 2 вҖӢ вүҘвҖҰвүҘ0 рқ‘ү рқ‘Ү V T ( рқ‘ӣ Г— рқ‘ӣ nГ—n) Right Singular Vectors Orthogonal: рқ‘ү рқ‘Ү рқ‘ү = рқҗј V T V=I Rows are eigenvectors of рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү рқҗҙ A T A Relationship to Eigendecomposition The singular values and singular vectors come from eigendecompositions of symmetric matrices: Left Singular Vectors рқҗҙ рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү = рқ‘Ҳ Оӣ рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘Ү AA T =UОӣU T рқ‘Ҳ U columns are eigenvectors of рқҗҙ рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү AA T Right Singular Vectors рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү рқҗҙ = рқ‘ү Оӣ рқ‘ү рқ‘Ү A T A=VОӣV T рқ‘ү V columns are eigenvectors of рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү рқҗҙ A T A Singular values = square roots of eigenvalues: рқңҺ рқ‘– = рқңҶ рқ‘– Пғ i вҖӢ = О» i вҖӢ вҖӢ Interactive: SVD Steps SVD decomposes any linear transformation into three operations: rotate, scale, then rotate. Watch how a unit circle transforms through each step. SVD Transformer Original Space рқ‘Ҙ x MATRIX CONSTRUCTOR VбөҖ (Input Rotation) -45В° ОЈ (Scaling) ПғвӮҒ 2 ПғвӮӮ 1 U (Output Rotation) 30В° CURRENT OPERATION Start with a unit circle. The orthonormal basis vectors (i, j) are aligned with the axes. Geometric Interpretation SVD tells us that every linear transformation, no matter how complex, is equivalent to three simple operations in sequence: 1 рқ‘ү рқ‘Ү V T (Rotation in Input Space) Rotates the coordinate system to align with the "principal axes" of the transformation. After this rotation, the transformation will only stretch along coordinate axes. 2 ОЈ ОЈ (Scaling) Stretches or compresses along the principal axes by the singular values. This is where the transformation does its "work." A unit circle becomes an ellipse whose semi-axes have lengths рқңҺ 1 , рқңҺ 2 Пғ 1 вҖӢ ,Пғ 2 вҖӢ , etc. 3 рқ‘Ҳ U (Rotation in Output Space) Final rotation that orients the scaled result to its output position. If the matrix changes dimension ( рқ‘ҡ вү  рқ‘ӣ m оҖ  =n), this rotation happens in the output space which may have different dimensions than input. Unit Circle to Ellipse When you apply any matrix A to a unit circle (or sphere in higher dimensions), the result is an ellipse (or ellipsoid). The singular values are the lengths of the semi-axes of this ellipse, and the singular vectors are the directions of these axes. Derivation How do we prove SVD exists and find it? The key insight is using рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү рқҗҙ A T A and рқҗҙ рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү AA T . Step 1: Form Symmetric Matrices For any рқҗҙ A, both рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү рқҗҙ A T A ( рқ‘ӣ Г— рқ‘ӣ nГ—n) and рқҗҙ рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү AA T ( рқ‘ҡ Г— рқ‘ҡ mГ—m) are symmetric positive semi-definite. Therefore they have: Real, non-negative eigenvalues Orthogonal eigenvectors Step 2: Key Identity If рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү рқҗҙ рқ‘Ј = рқңҶ рқ‘Ј A T Av=О»v, then рқҗҙ рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү ( рқҗҙ рқ‘Ј ) = рқҗҙ ( рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү рқҗҙ рқ‘Ј ) = рқҗҙ ( рқңҶ рқ‘Ј ) = рқңҶ ( рқҗҙ рқ‘Ј ) AA T (Av)=A(A T Av)=A(О»v)=О»(Av) So if рқ‘Ј v is an eigenvector of рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү рқҗҙ A T A with eigenvalue рқңҶ О», then рқҗҙ рқ‘Ј Av is an eigenvector of рқҗҙ рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү AA T with the same eigenvalue рқңҶ О». Step 3: Define Components рқ‘ү V (Right Vectors) Eigenvectors of рқҗҙ рқ‘Ү рқҗҙ A T A, normalized. ОЈ ОЈ (Singular Values) рқңҺ рқ‘– = рқңҶ рқ‘– Пғ i вҖӢ = О» i вҖӢ вҖӢ рқ‘Ҳ U (Left Vectors) рқ‘ў рқ‘– = 1 рқңҺ рқ‘– рқҗҙ рқ‘Ј рқ‘– u i вҖӢ = Пғ i вҖӢ 1 вҖӢ Av i вҖӢ Verification With these definitions, рқҗҙ рқ‘Ј рқ‘– = рқңҺ рқ‘– рқ‘ў рқ‘– Av i вҖӢ =Пғ i вҖӢ u i вҖӢ for each рқ‘– i. In matrix form: рқҗҙ рқ‘ү = рқ‘Ҳ ОЈ AV=UОЈ, which rearranges to рқҗҙ = рқ‘Ҳ ОЈ рқ‘ү рқ‘Ү A=UОЈV T (since рқ‘ү V is orthogonal, рқ‘ү вҲ’ 1 = рқ‘ү рқ‘Ү V вҲ’1 =V T ). Truncated SVD (Low-Rank Approximation) The real power of SVD is not in exact factorization but in approximation. By keeping only the top k singular values, we get the best rank-k approximation of A. рқҗҙ рқ‘ҳ = вҲ‘ рқ‘– = 1 рқ‘ҳ рқңҺ рқ‘– рқ‘ў рқ‘– рқ‘Ј рқ‘– рқ‘Ү A k вҖӢ =вҲ‘ i=1 k вҖӢ Пғ i вҖӢ u i вҖӢ v i T вҖӢ This is the sum of k rank-1 matrices, each scaled by its singular value. Eckart-Young Theorem (Optimality) Among all rank- рқ‘ҳ k matrices, рқҗҙ рқ‘ҳ A k вҖӢ minimizes the Frobenius norm of the error: вҲЈ вҲЈ рқҗҙ вҲ’ рқҗҙ рқ‘ҳ вҲЈ вҲЈ рқҗ№ = рқңҺ рқ‘ҳ + 1 2 + рқңҺ рқ‘ҳ + 2 2 + вҖҰ + рқңҺ рқ‘ҹ 2 вҲЈвҲЈAвҲ’A k вҖӢ вҲЈвҲЈ F вҖӢ = Пғ k+1 2 вҖӢ +Пғ k+2 2 вҖӢ +вҖҰ+Пғ r 2 вҖӢ вҖӢ The error equals the sum of squared discarded singular values! This is why keeping the largest singular values is optimal. Compressing a 1000Г—1000 Image Original: 1,000,000 pixels. SVD (k=50): 50 left vectors + 50 singular values + 50 right vectors = 50 Г— (1000 + 1 + 1000) = 100,050 numbers. Result: 90% space reduction with minimal visual loss! Interactive: Low-Rank Approximation Drag the slider to see how much "energy" (information) is captured by keeping different numbers of singular values. Low-Rank Approx Reconstructing a matrix using only its most important patterns. Rank (k) 1 1 (Abstract) 8 (Exact) INFORMATION KEPT 74.7% Пғ1 = 50.0 Пғ2 = 25.0 Пғ3 = 12.0 Пғ4 = 8.0 Пғ5 = 2.7 Пғ6 = 2.0 Пғ7 = 1.6 Пғ8 = 1.3 Singular Value Spectrum Notice how Rank 1 captures the main gradient, Rank 2 splits it, and Rank 3 adds texture. Rank 4 adds the central bump. ORIGINAL MATRIX The full data matrix (Target). SVD(k=1) RECONSTRUCTION (AK) Approximation using top 1 patterns. RESIDUALS (ERROR) What's left behind (Noise). + Value - Value Zero Key Properties Rank Number of non-zero singular values. Frobenius Norm Sum of squared entries = sum of squared singular values. вҲЈ вҲЈ рқҗҙ вҲЈ вҲЈ рқҗ№ = вҲ‘ рқңҺ рқ‘– 2 вҲЈвҲЈAвҲЈвҲЈ F вҖӢ = вҲ‘Пғ i 2 вҖӢ вҖӢ Spectral Norm (2-Norm) Maximum stretching factor = largest singular value. вҲЈ вҲЈ рқҗҙ вҲЈ вҲЈ 2 = рқңҺ 1 вҲЈвҲЈAвҲЈвҲЈ 2 вҖӢ =Пғ 1 вҖӢ Condition Number Measures numerical stability. рқң… ( рқҗҙ ) = рқңҺ 1 / рқңҺ рқ‘ҹ Оә(A)=Пғ 1 вҖӢ /Пғ r вҖӢ Pseudo-Inverse (Moore-Penrose) How do you invert a non-square matrix? SVD gives the answer: рқҗҙ + = рқ‘ү ОЈ + рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘Ү A + =VОЈ + U T Where ОЈ + ОЈ + is formed by taking reciprocals of non-zero singular values ( 1 / рқңҺ рқ‘– 1/Пғ i вҖӢ ) and transposing. This solves least squares problems: рқ‘Ҙ = рқҗҙ + рқ‘Ҹ x=A + b. Applications Image Compression An image is a matrix of pixel values. Keep top k singular values, discard the rest. The image remains recognizable even with k much smaller than rank. Recommendation Systems User-item rating matrix is sparse. SVD finds latent factors. Predicted rating = рқ‘Ҳ рқ‘ҳ ОЈ рқ‘ҳ рқ‘ү рқ‘ҳ рқ‘Ү U k вҖӢ ОЈ k вҖӢ V k T вҖӢ . This fills in missing entries. (Netflix Prize key component). Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA) Document-term matrix: rows = documents, columns = words. SVD reveals "topics" as latent dimensions. Documents and words are embedded in the same semantic space. Noise Reduction Noise typically appears in small singular values (random fluctuations don't create strong patterns). Truncating small singular values removes noise while keeping signal. SVD vs PCA vs Eigendecomposition Method Matrix Type What It Finds Main Use Eigendecomposition Square ( рқ‘ӣ Г— рқ‘ӣ nГ—n) Eigenvalues & eigenvectors Theoretical analysis, dynamics SVD Any ( рқ‘ҡ Г— рқ‘ӣ mГ—n) Singular values & vectors Data compression, inversion PCA Data matrix Principal components Dimensionality reduction PCA via SVD PCA is often computed using SVD rather than eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix: Center data matrix X Compute SVD: X = U ОЈ V^T Principal components = columns of V Projected data = U ОЈ This is more numerically stable than forming X^T X explicitly. Previous: Positive Definite Matrices Next: PCA

The foundation of modern data compression. Introduction Eigendecomposition is powerful, but it has a fatal flaw: it only works on square matrices. Real-world data matrices are rarely square. We might have m users and n movies, or m documents and n words. Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is the generalization that works on any matrix, regardless of shape. Understanding PCA also helps, as PCA is often computed using SVD. The Movie Rating Intuition Consider a matrix A where rows are Users and columns are Movies. Entry A[i,j] is user i's rating of movie j.
